responseSet.title: The role of the Revenge Cycle in the Conflict in Gaza
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The role of the Revenge Cycle in the Conflict in Gaza

David Lotto

David Lotto

Email: dlotto@nycap.rr.com

David Lotto  

Gaza, revenge, narcissistic rage, Kohut

This paper examines the psychological roots of the war in Gaza using Kohut's theory of narcissistic injury and the vengeful reaction it engenders. This is followed by an exploration of certain aspects of revenge, including some brain imaging findings, and its relationship to justice. The importance of humiliation in feeding the cycle of narcissistic injury leading to revenge, leading to further humiliation, leading to further revenge, is discussed. The cycle can be self-perpetuating as we are currently witnessing in Gaza.

[[RunningHead]]

Dissociation and Trauma in the Context of Mental Health Responses for those Populations Affected by Severe Adversity and Suffering During Warfare: Adopting a Socio-Cultural Perspective on Israel-Palestine

Anne Jennings

Anne Jennings

Essex, UK
Email: a.m.jennings@talk21.com

Anne Jennings  

warfare, dissociation, trauma, mental health, socio-cultural context

In this short article, I consider questions of dissociation and trauma for populations facing severe adverse situations within warzones, focussing on Israel-Palestine. As Psychoanalytic Psychotherapists interested in global mental health, attention to the human conditions and safety needed for clinical interventions in war-torn and occupied regions is paramount. Consideration of the cultural lens through which we view psychological distress, will help us to respond constructively and sensitively to the mental health needs of all those directly affected by the conflict. This article focusses on the realities for Palestinian clinicians working to support mental health in Gaza, who speak to the importance of understanding the political and social situation that gives rise to suffering in Palestine and the need for a culturally informed response to the mass psychological suffering of Palestinian communities. Such a widening of our understanding as a discipline will help us to respond constructively and sensitively to the mental health needs of all peoples affected. We may also be encouraged to assume a more active humanitarian stance in supporting the cessation of killing by calling for an immediate ceasefire to enable all communities to exist and to thrive.

[[RunningHead]]

With so Many Conflicts, Why have a Special issue on the War in Israel/Palestine?

John, Lord Alderdice

John, Lord Alderdice

Email: john.alderdice@hmc.ox.ac.uk
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-4322-0049

John, Lord Alderdice  

The world is currently living through the most dangerous period of war and violent political conflict since the end of the Second World War. Indeed, with the advent of hypersonic weapons, able to carry a nuclear payload, and potentially managed by artificial intelligence, this has become the most dangerous period in human history. For the psychoanalytic world too, it is a time of deep division and profound danger.

Prior to the First World War, Freud and his colleagues focussed largely on the material that emerged in individual psychoanalytical work, with the sexual instinct foremost. However, such was the upheaval and torment across Europe during the 1914-1918 war that they had to pay attention to the psychology of societal disturbance and, going ‘beyond the pleasure principle’, developed psychoanalytic theory to address the aggressive drive, and postulated a death instinct. In the years that followed more was written about the societal applications of psychoanalysis, but then the world was plunged into a second global cataclysm. There were many horrors, not least the ending of the war with the first use of nuclear weapons, but the awfulness of the Holocaust had a very particular impact on psychoanalysts and their professional communities because many of them were Jewish. Some survived in continental Europe but many more fled to Britain, the USA and elsewhere for safety – Freud and his daughter Anna, seeking refuge in London. After such terror and trauma many psychoanalysts turned away from thinking so much about societal relations, focussing entirely on their clinical work, as a reaction to their terrible experiences. They had learned what can happen in the larger society when dangerous movements develop with the terrifying and seemingly irresistible destructive force of a tsunami.

For some Jews these traumas gave momentum to the Zionist movement that sought to create a state which would be a safe and secure place for Jews. Despite the exile having lasted for almost two thousand years, the emotional pull of the ancient historical home in Palestine was so great that it overcame any rational considerations of whether this small sliver of land in the Middle East was likely to be a truly secure space. responseSet.title: Editorial
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Paul Cundy

Paul Cundy

London, UK
paulcundy@protonmail.com

Paul Cundy  

This special issue focuses on the conflict between Israel and Gaza. Whilst at the time of writing this editorial we are in the early days of a ceasefire, there is a widely held fear that this will not be the end of the conflict. Similarly, although the horrific events of 7/10/23 were the catalyst for this most recent episode, they were certainly not the beginning of the conflict.

According to Wikipedia (2025), ‘as of 8 January, over 47,000 people − 45,936 Palestinian and 1,706 Israeli – have been reported killed in the Gaza War, as well as 166 journalists and media workers, 120 academics, and over 224 humanitarian aid workers, a number that includes 179 employees of UNWRA. Scholars have estimated 80% of Palestinians killed are civilians, while a study by OCHR, that verified fatalities from three independent sources, found that 70% of Palestinians killed were women and children’. Tragically, research published in the Lancet suggests that the Palestinian death toll could be 55,298–78,525, substantially higher than the official figures reported by the Hamas-run health ministry (Jamaluddine et al., 2025).

In addition to this unimaginable human cost, the war has revealed deep divisions within the global community. Although most Western governments have supported Israel, there has also been extensive pro-Palestinian activism within these countries. These divisions have also been apparent within our profession, causing rifts between patient and clinician, between colleagues and within our professional organisations. Divisions are not uncommon in our work but this conflict, more than any other, seems to lead to such extreme polarisation that we become unable to think together. Thought and speech are shut down by accusations of hatred or prejudice. This journal has experienced such dynamics, both between colleagues and between the journal and its readership. These challenges to thinking and the policing of discourse were one of the factors that led us to conclude that the conflict between Israel and Gaza, rather than conflicts in general, would be an appropriate subject for a special issue of this journal. But, more importantly, it would have been collusive to remain silent during a conflict that is alleged to involve war crimes and genocide.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Understanding Evil: A Psychoanalytic Perspective on the Hamas Attacks of 7 October 2023

Aner Govrin

Aner Govrin

Ramat Gan, Israel
Email: govrina.biu@gmail.com

Aner Govrin  

Evil, victim, perpetrator, conflict, Israel-Palestine conflict

This paper examines the Hamas attacks of 7 October 2023, through the lens of several theories of evil. It applies frameworks from Christopher Bollas, Ruth Stein, Sue Grand, Robert J. Lifton, and Aner Govrin to explore the psychological underpinnings of this act of terror. The study employs an interpretative approach to analyze the attacks, considering the perpetrators’ backgrounds and the sociopolitical context. Key concepts explored include Bollas's ‘killed self’, Stein's analysis of religious justification for violence, Grand's ‘malignant dissociative contagion’, Lifton's theory of totalism, and Govrin's prototype of evil perception. The paper discusses how these theories illuminate the role of trauma, ideology, and psychological distortion in fostering extreme violence. It also considers the ethical implications of applying psychoanalytic theories to large-scale events and acknowledges the limitations of this approach. The analysis reveals common threads across theories, including the impact of psychological damage, disconnection from empathy, and the interplay of love and hate in acts of evil. The paper concludes by emphasizing the importance of recognizing suffering on all sides of the conflict while maintaining a clear moral stance against acts of terror. It calls for further interdisciplinary research to inform strategies for conflict resolution and prevention of future atrocities.

[[RunningHead]]

Transitioning from Soldier's Matrix to the Development of Negative Capability

Anna Zajenkowska , Agnieszka Chrzczonowicz-Stępień and Katarzyna Czajkowska-Łukasiewicz

Katarzyna Czajkowska-Łukasiewicz

Warsaw, Poland
Email: zajenkowska@gmail.com

Warsaw, Poland

Katarzyna Czajkowska-Łukasiewicz  

Negative capability, regressed relations, chosen traumas, collective narcissism, social identity theory, narcissistic injuries and rage, soldier's matrix

In the 21st century, the concept of negative capability—defined as the capacity to tolerate uncertainty and engage in deep reflection rather than reacting impulsively—has become increasingly relevant. This paper explores how transgenerational trauma, particularly in the context of Israeli-Palestinian and other global conflicts, undermines this crucial capability. Such trauma often leads to narcissistic injuries and rage, further eroding negative capability at both individual and collective levels. We examine the pervasive feelings of insecurity and threat, introducing the concept of the “soldier's matrix,” a psychological framework driven by these emotions. This sets the stage for an analysis of reactions to traumatic events, linking narcissistic dynamics with deeper relational traumas. To clarify this complex interplay, we draw on concepts such as regressed relations, chosen traumas, collective narcissism, and social identity theory. We also focus on diagnosing the factors that hinder mentalization and obstruct the restoration of negative capability. Finally, we propose a comprehensive three-layered intervention for conflicted parties, emphasizing the need to rebuild reflective spaces through education, international cooperation, and leadership from political and social figures.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Psychoanalytic Reflections on the Events in Gaza

David Bell

David Bell

London, UK
Email: davidlbell@hotmail.com

David Bell  

politics, Palestine, psychoanalysis, Israel

This paper explores the different ways in which psychoanalysis can engage with socio-political events, taking as its primary focus the conflict between Israel and Palestine/Gaza. At a general level psychoanalysis shares with other disciplines a mode of thought which emphasises such things as historical continuity, the distinction between what is manifest and the hidden realities it obscures. More specifically psychoanalytic contribution comes from its understanding of the nature and functioning of unconscious phantasy both at group and individual level. The paper problematises the frequent misuse of the term ‘psychoanalytic neutrality’ which, the author claims, serves to silence opposition to a dominant ideology rather than question it. Lastly, the author describes how attempts to think about the onset of the current conflict in its long historical and geopolitical context are misunderstood as seeking to provide justification- the author emphasises the need to maintain the critical distinction between these two modalities of thought.

[[RunningHead]]

When Truth is Not Shared

Dana Amir

Dana Amir

Haifa, Israel
Email: dana.amir2@gmail.com

Dana Amir  

trauma, evil, minus links, forgiveness, Bion, truth

Political violence has, for a very long time, been a focus of academic interest in various disciplines, probing the relentlessly cyclic nature of violence and counter-violence. This essay looks at the Israeli-Palestinian conflict with reference to the map of links by means of which Bion conceptualized the relations between two individuals, giving special attention to how negative configurations of these links impede contact with truth on both sides. Relating to theoretical writings by Baudrillard, Benjamin, and Arendt, this essay suggests replacing planar with spatial thinking and the condition of revenge with that of forgiveness.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Vulnerable Encounters: Identities and their Discontents

Yael Bankirer

Yael Bankirer

Email: yaelbankirer@gmail.com

Yael Bankirer  

Israel-Gaza, trauma, witnessing, mutual vulnerability, identities

In this paper, I try to articulate my psychoanalytic-political-emotional experience as an Israeli psychoanalyst working in England during this painful and challenging time. With a growing awareness of how difficult it is to hold an analytic space in the face of traumatic violence, I analyse the places in which dialogues collapse into a binary framework, both in my clinical encounters and in the discourse itself. Connecting Amir's concept of the Inner Witness, Benjamin's understanding of the Moral Third, and Butler's Compass of Mourning, I aim to explore vulnerabilities beyond the binary structure of doer and done-to, victim and aggressor. Recognising a repeated denial of pain and loss at the heart of our discourse, I argue for a political-psychoanalytic stance that can hold a more complex picture by agreeing to witness.

Poetry and Re-Imagining in a Time of Crisis

‘Changing the Very Terms of the Dilemma’

Mary Adams

Mary Adams

London, UK
Email: Mary.adams@btinternet.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-2330-0992

Mary Adams  

Re-imagining, Palestine, Nakba, Heaney, Eghbariah

The author compares the power of imagery in poetry with the unconscious images and re-imagining that allow us to see things in a new light in psychotherapy. Palestinian poetry illuminates their world and the devastation happening in Gaza in the way dreams capture our inner world. Emphasis is given to the role of the imagination in effecting social change, seen remarkably in the ‘Good Friday Agreement’ in Northern Ireland. The poetry of Seamus Heaney, for example, is linked with a fundamental perceptual shift around issues of national sovereignty. In his long-term support for Palestine, Heaney looked to poetry to redress the injustice and to counter the silencing and ‘turning a blind eye’. Nakba denial is seen as attacking the very life sustaining act of re-imagining.

Confronting the Inhuman

Paul Hoggett

Paul Hoggett

Bristol, UK
Email: paul.hoggett@uwe.ac.uk

Paul Hoggett  

indifference, cruelty, ecocide, genocide, identitarianism

This article uses the lens of both Gaza and the climate crisis to examine some aspects of inhumanity. In both situations, we observe a puzzling indifference to ongoing manifestations of human destructiveness. The article explores the dynamics of disavowal, the complex nature of racism, and the tragic dimension of some political conflicts. In conclusion, some of the psychological and material forces involved in the resurgence of authoritarian nationalism are considered.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Brief Report: Private Pleasures and the Damage Done: Asking the Unasked Question about Consumption of Online Pornography

Alessandra Lemma

Alessandra Lemma

London, UK
Email: alemma@mac.com

Alessandra Lemma  

online pornography, sexuality, harm, mentalisation of sexual desire

The domestication of the Internet has invigorated debate about the harms of pornography. The difference between pre-Internet and online pornography is not in any straightforward sense only one of degree. Compared to pre-Internet pornography, speed, and ease of access to an extensive range of pornographic content, along with the consumption of pornography in a virtual context, have introduced new prudential risks. In this Brief Report I restrict my focus to the impact on sexual health and on personal relationships and summarise the relevant research. Drawing on a psychoanalytically informed conceptual framework, I argue that the online medium changes, in prudentially significant ways, the consumer’s relationship to the sexual materials by providing a virtual space within which sexual desire is gratified quickly and non-reflectively, undermining the consumer’s capacity a) to mentalise their sexual desire and that of the other and b) to evaluate the prudential risks associated with the consumption of online pornography. The risks are especially significant for the digital generation whose sexual development is now more likely to be shaped by online pornography. I conclude by outlining one key practice implication arising from the research.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

When Florence Met Freud: Interaction and Intersection between Psychoanalysis and Nursing

Sarah M. Ramsey

Sarah M. Ramsey

London, UK
http://orcid.org/0000-0001-8068-8439

Moorside Rd, Davyhulme, Manchester M41 5SL, UK
Email: sarah.ramsey@mft.nhs.uk
http://orcid.org/0000-0001-8068-8439

Sarah M. Ramsey  

psychoanalysis, nursing, healthcare, psychosocial

A theoretical paper which considers the previous multiple attempts by nursing to engage with and usefully apply the theories of psychoanalysis to varying aspects of the nursing role. References to psychoanalysis within the nursing literature of the last century are reviewed and the legitimacy of the use of psychoanalytic theory ‘outside the clinic’ explored with reference to general hospital nursing in the UK’s National Health Service. Themes explored include unconscious motivation behind nursing as a career choice, with associated risks of stress and burnout when the unconscious drive to heal is thwarted. Psychoanalytic consideration of nurse-patient relationships provides insight into the patient experience on hospital wards, whilst exploration of other aspects of the nursing role was found to be limited. From a wider perspective, psychoanalysis offers nursing an insight into societal changes impacting the profession. The systems and processes of the healthcare institution have also been explored, particularly in relation to containment of anxiety, though limited practical impact has resulted from the psychoanalytic insight encountered in the literature. The limited engagement between psychoanalysis and general nursing is considered from both a psychoanalytic and nursing perspective; ongoing potentiality for enriching dialogue between the disciplines is established and potential barriers explored.

[[RunningHead]]

Evaluating the 20-Day Dynamic Interpersonal Therapy (DIT) Training Programme: A Mixed Methods Investigation to Identify Characteristics Associated with Successful Outcomes of DIT

Jennifer Khalastchy , Deborah Abrahams , Rob Saunders and Peter Fonagy

Peter Fonagy

London, UK
Email: jennykhala@yahoo.com

London, UK

London, UK

Peter Fonagy  

Dynamic interpersonal therapy, depression, brief psychodynamic therapy, psychoanalytic/dynamic competences, thematic analysis

Dynamic Interpersonal Therapy (DIT) is a brief psychodynamic therapy for depression, developed from the fundamental competences of evidence-based psychoanalytic/dynamic therapeutic methods. The 20-Day DIT training programme at the Anna Freud Centre in London is offered for clinicians who do not meet these psychoanalytic/dynamic competences. 28 therapists, each with three patients, underwent the 20-Day DIT training in 2019. The current study evaluated the 20-Day DIT training by investigating factors associated with the successful patient outcomes of this cohort. Reliable recovery and reliable improvement of patients was 69% and 90%, respectively. Quantitative analyses found that patients who have positive outcomes were likely to have lower baseline Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9) scores and lower patient complexity. A thematic analysis of the therapists’ perspectives suggested that the most important elements of DIT perceived to be associated with successful patient outcomes cluster around four superordinate themes: aspects of the DIT model/training, characteristics of the patient, aspects of the patient-therapist relationship, and characteristics of the therapist. The results suggest that the DIT protocol can be acquired by novice therapists in psychoanalytic/dynamic methods and offer insight into the factors that may be associated with successful outcomes.

Testing Heinz Kohut’s thoughts on Narcissism and Narcissistic Rage: Narcissistic Injury Paves the Way for Radicalization and Subclinical Paranoid States – An Experimental Study

Klaus Michael Reininger , Hannah Marie Biel , Timo Hennig , Steffen Moritz , Peer Briken and Bernd Löwe

Bernd Löwe

Hamburg, Germany
Martinistr. 52, Hamburg D-20246, Germany
m.reininger@uke.uni-hamburg.de
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-6671-0465

Hamburg, Germany

Potsdam, Germany

http://orcid.org/0000-0003-4220-3378

Hamburg, Germany
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-4220-3378

Bernd Löwe  

Radicalization, state paranoia, narcissism, narcissistic injury, narcissistic rage

According to psychoanalytic self-psychological theory, narcissistic injuries may induce radical intentions, and subclinical paranoid states via self-psychological mechanisms. The testing took place in a narcissistic-injuring situation in Germany, where a reform of psychotherapy-training was implemented with only brief period of transition for those aiming to become psychotherapists after graduating. Within a randomized controlled-experimental design, 100 psychology-students were to read information about short transition-periods for them (affectedness-by-the-reform-condition) vs. for education-students (no-affectedness-by-the-reform-condition). After reading a radicalization-prompt, discussing in a 30-minute-group-discussion, participants in both conditions completed dependent measures of separatist identification, negative affects, overconfidence, radical intentions and subclinical paranoid states. Compared to the control condition (n = 49), psychologists in the experimental-condition (n = 51) stronger experienced narcissistic injury (d between 0.79 and 1.01; manipulation-check). They experienced a stronger alloyance with an archaic-omnipotent object (d = 0.52), exhibited more negative affects (d = 0.46), claimed overconfidence (d = 0.50), more radical intentions (d = 0.52), and subclinical paranoid states (d = 0.43). Our study supports Kohut’s idea that antisocial as well as paranoid states are responses to narcissistic injuries mediated by the alloyance with an archaic-omnipotent object, negative affects and overconfidence.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Paul Cundy

Paul Cundy

London, UK
paulcundy@protonmail.com

Paul Cundy  

Welcome to the first issue of the Journal for 2024, which begins with a research article by Klaus Reininger, Hannah Biel, Timo Hennig, Steffen Moritz, Peer Briken and Bernd Löwe entitled ‘Testing Heinz Kohut’s thoughts on narcissism and narcissistic rage: narcissistic injury paves the way for radicalization and subclinical paranoid states – an experimental study’. The authors identified a situation in Germany that would enable them to conduct research into the consequences of narcissistic injury. In 2019 German authorities began implementing a structural reform of psychotherapy training, with only a brief period of transition for those who were mid-training during the reforms. The authors conducted a randomised controlled experiment in which 100 psychology students were allocated to either a group that read information about how the short transition period would affect their own path to qualification as psychotherapists (experimental condition), or to a group that read about how the short transition period would affect education students hoping to qualify as child and adolescent psychotherapists (control condition). Both groups then also read the views of a professor who believed that effective, immediate action would be necessary to overturn the proposed short transition periods (a radicalization prompt). Following a 30 minute group discussion, participants in both conditions completed dependent measures of separatist identification, negative affects, overconfidence, radical intentions and subclinical paranoid states. Compared to the control condition, psychologists in the experimental condition experienced greater narcissistic injury. They experienced a stronger alloyance with an archaic omnipotent object, exhibited more negative affects, claimed overconfidence, more radical intentions and subclinical paranoid states. The authors suggest that these results support Kohut’s idea that narcissistic injuries can lead to antisocial and/or paranoid states mediated by the alloyance with an archaic-omnipotent object, negative affects and overconfidence.

[[RunningHead]]

Clinician Experiences of Remotely Delivering Psychodynamic and Intensive Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy within an NHS Institution During the COVID-19 Pandemic, and Possible Implications for Future Clinical Practice

Anna Croxford , Alan Baban , Sasha Kreel and Julian Stern

Julian Stern

London, UK
anna.croxford1@nhs.net

London, UK

London, UK

Julian Stern  

COVID-19, psychoanalytic psychotherapy, psychodynamic psychotherapy, remote

When it became clear that the COVID-19 pandemic would require significant shifts in working practice, staff in the Adult Complex Needs department at the Tavistock clinic designed a study to explore the emotional impact of remote working on clinicians and patients, and to consider related clinical implications both individually and collectively. A set of semi-structured questions were designed to tap into experiences of working remotely, and clinicians were interviewed. The material arising from these interviews has been summarised into themes. The predominant theme was clinicians’ awareness of the importance of their own emotional milieu in delivering psychoanalytic psychotherapy, especially at a time of crisis, in their initial emotional responses and as they gathered further remote experience. They raised challenges around maintaining an analytic frame amid therapeutic uncertainties, and in the context of the many physical losses of a remote frame: blurred clinical boundaries, altered emotional experiences and resonance, and important impacts on holding, patient attendance, and worsening distress and risks for patients who struggled remotely. Strategies to adapt were key, particularly remaining emotionally consistent and observant amidst uncertainty, and support was paramount to do so. These experiences assist in informing clinicians about the limitations and uses of remote clinical work.

responseSet.title: A Virtual Frame – How Does Forensic Psychotherapy Work Remotely?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Virtual Frame – How Does Forensic Psychotherapy Work Remotely?

Gemma Routledge

Gemma Routledge

London, UK
gjroutledge@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0009-0008-8776-8595

UK

Gemma Routledge  

forensic psychotherapy, remote video psychotherapy, telemental health

Psychotherapy has been practised via remote technology since the mid-1990s and has grown in mainstream popularity; yet, it remains controversial, particularly for psychoanalytically informed treatment, and limited research in this area has been published. However, COVID-19 provoked a necessary transition to online working across the psychotherapy profession, including clinicians providing psychoanalytic treatment to a unique forensic outpatient group remotely for the first time. This qualitative research study sought insights from this sample via grounded theory interviews and analysis to inform hypothetical future practice of remote psychotherapy via videoconferencing to forensic populations with limited access to treatment. This generated a substantive theory about how forensic psychotherapy works remotely: In the context of a forced transition to remote working, forensic psychotherapists make attempts to substitute for real by establishing an alternative frame; noticing how differently contact is mediated; adapting technique; and evaluating suitability for remote treatment. An implication of these findings may be that developing a new Virtual Frame for forensic psychotherapy, supported by specialist training and supervision, could increase its reach within settings that provide sufficient containment for the work. Further research is encouraged to support this undertaking and consider which patients, and/or clinicians, might work effectively this way.

[[RunningHead]]

Experience of Psychic Restructuring among Refugees in the Context of Life in Refuge

Riswana B and Baiju Gopal

Baiju Gopal

Bengaluru, India
riswananasweef@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-1678-3658

Bengaluru, India
http://orcid.org/0000-0001-8191-7045

Baiju Gopal  

Art-based therapy, exile, psychic restructuring, life in the refuge, first-generation

Changes in the inner voice, thoughts, and feelings due to the recurrent experiences in social and cultural reality are defined as psychic restructuring (PR). Any collective trauma can drastically change a person’s psyche in multiple ways. The first argument of the study is that PR happens in individuals as a result of exile, which has manifold reasons. The second argument is that first-generation older adults crave for their homeland more intensely because of their subtle memories of the left behind. The third argument is that there are metaphorical representations of exilic experiences in the art forms that would depict their inexplicable quest for PR. Finally, it suggests the possibility of constructive PR in refugees with the aid of clinically driven and standardized art-based therapies (ABT). These arguments have multicollinearity in the context of PR, which still needs to be studied. The study tries to bridge the gap or lacuna in this research arena.

[[RunningHead]]

Learning from the Patient in Retrospect: Patrick Casement’s Clinical Approach in an Extraordinary Paragraph

Marc Kingsley and Patrick Casement

Patrick Casement

Harley Therapy 10 Harley Street, London W1G 9PF, UK
marc.kingsley@nelft.nhs.uk
http://orcid.org/0000-0001-8363-927X

London, UK

Patrick Casement  

Psychoanalytic psychotherapy, learning from patients, unconscious communication, unconscious hope, trial identification

(Received 3 October 2023; accepted 17 February 2024)

Patrick Casement, renowned psychoanalyst, was recently encouraged by Dr Kelly Jayne, to write a paragraph describing his approach to clinical work. ‘Learning from the patient in retrospect’ was published in the Bulletin of the British Psychoanalytical Society June 2023 (vol 59 no 5) page 31. As the Bulletin is confidential, and only for members of the British Psychoanalytical Society, we are pleased to share it to a wider audience here, with kind permission of the author and publisher.

‘Now approaching 88, I had thought I would be immune to further temptations to write. But I was mistaken. When asked to describe my approach to clinical work “in just one paragraph”, how could I resist such a challenge? So, here goes.

Learning from the Patient in Retrospect

While listening to a patient, there are some questions which I am putting to myself. What might the patient most need me to hear in what they are communicating to me at this moment, whether in word or silence, or in the quality of their presence? What can I say that could open up some exploring of this? What might I then be contributing to the analytic space and process? Could this then lead to creative playfulness or might it just lead to some confrontation or compliance? Am I merely applying insight from preconception or am I opening up a joint exploring towards some fresh understanding, which could be arrived at along with the patient? Am I trying to analyse from some position of assumed understanding, or am I truly open to arriving at some insight along with the patient? Am I actually open to learning with my patient at this moment or am I slipping back into trying to interpret from theory and from other experience?’

The paragraph was warmly received, with many colleagues sharing generous feedback some of which we include below.

[[RunningHead]]

Lacan on Depression and Melancholia, edited by Derek Hook and Stijn Vanheule, New York, NY, Routledge, 2023, 193 pp., $35.95 (pback), ISBN 9781032106533

Emma Lieber

Emma Lieber

Elieber14@gmail.com

Emma Lieber  

It doesn’t take a psychoanalyst to tell you that depression is in the air. Read any newspaper or magazine and you’ll see a proliferation of articles about contemporary mental health crises, often among teens complaining of anxiety and depression; or about various celebrities’ struggles with depression, experiences proclaimed and disseminated for the sake of so-called normalization; or about the global and national upsurge in the use of antidepressants, depression’s corollary. Like anxiety, depression is something we all seem to ‘have’, or have had at one time or another; the current cultural context (pandemic, war, economic upheaval, climate crisis) is undoubtedly a factor. In their editors’ introduction to Lacan on Depression and Melancholia, Derek Hook and Stijn Vanheule are appropriately skeptical of the cultural deployment of depression as a concept, one that is ‘used by so many people, in so many different ways, that it has become virtually impossible to dislodge, to effectively question or critique’ (p. 1). In Lacanian terms, ‘depression’ has become a ‘master signifier’, a cultural quilting point that obviates its own exegesis, and that thus ‘falls short of clinical or conceptual rigor’ even within its very promotion and establishment of the so-called mental health fields. The spread of ‘depression’ as a marketing tool and handy diagnosis is, in fact, itself a depressive phenomenon, a symptom of a broader discontent ‘within contemporary Western culture’ (p. 4) and a kind of melancholic refusal of lack that itself performs as much as it designates the matter at hand. Perhaps indeed ‘depression’ as a signifier – or at least its repeated appearance in media and medical circulation – is a better means of diagnosing a culture than a person.

And yet, certainly, the book – after offering this framing gesture, by which a Lacanian perspective allows the editors to attend to the status of depression as a cultural signifier rather than as a signified – by no means goes on to dismiss depression as an object of Lacanian attention, or the Lacanian epistemology as a frame for its investigation; obviously, the claim of the book is that a Lacanian theoretical viewpoint can open something up for clinicians about depression as a subjective experience (if not as a discrete diagnostic category). This despite the fact that, as both the editors and many of the contributors remind us (and unlike anxiety, which he theorized at length), Lacan had relatively little to say about depression beyond famously calling it a ‘moral failing’. [[RunningHead]]

Review of the 6th Annual Stuart Hall Public Conversation with Jaqueline Rose

Victoria Barker

Victoria Barker

London, United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland
drvictoriabarker@gmail.com

Victoria Barker  

(Received 13 August 2023; accepted 14 August 2023)

Serendipitously I found myself within the space of a few months reading the 1992 paper by Stuart Hall ‘New Ethnicities’ (Hall, 1992) for a journal club discussion, viewing the Tate Britain exhibition ‘Sixty Years: The Unfinished Conversation’ which tracks the work of Stuart Hall through the film work of Sir John Akomfrah, and sitting in the audience of ‘The 6th Annual Stuart Hall Public Conversation with Jaqueline Rose’ which happened to take place in the anniversary month of his birth and death. Perhaps it was not simple coincidence, this presence of Hall at this time. His work speaks of intersectionality, identity, diaspora and dislocation; finding a place where you can be yourself and equally be part of something wider, and these are fundamental themes for our current fractured political and social context.

The 6th Annual Stuart Hall Public Conversation began with a paper presented by Jacqueline Rose entitled ‘What is a Subject? Politics and Psyche after Stuart Hall’ and was followed by a discussion with Dr Sharon Numa, Psychoanalyst and Fellow of the British Psychoanalytical Society. Rose, who is a Professor of Humanities and co-director of the Birkbeck Institute for the Humanities, University of London and Associate Member of the British Institute of Psychoanalysis, gave a wide-ranging discourse on the work of Stuart Hall, starting with his early experience of witnessing the migrants from Jamaica and the Caribbean disembarking at Paddington station and tumbling out onto the streets of London not long after he himself had arrived in the UK. This gave him pause to reflect on his migrant identity and the things that tied him and distanced him from this group of hopefuls as they stepped into this new world. Rose highlighted the point that they carried with them the ‘Transgenerational Histories’ encrypted in their unconscious, those histories of colonialism, slavery and racism lying dormant ready to be re-enacted in their lives and thus passed on to the next generation. What Rose did not expand on and what Numa subsequently picked up on in their conversation was how this forms the core of a psychoanalytic understanding of the development of the individual and underpins how psychoanalysis can help us to explore and potentially address these social injustices. [[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Paul Cundy

Paul Cundy

London, UK
paulcundy@protonmail.com

Paul Cundy  

In early April this year, shortly before writing this editorial, the paper copy of 37-04 was delivered (4 months after the cover month of December). Upon receipt, several readers were prompted to write and share their concerns about the first paragraph of the editorial of that special issue entitled ‘Time to (re)-integrate Dissociation into Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy’. Writing in the first 10 days after the terrorist attack on Israel, Dr Shahar had described some impressions of the population’s response and his role providing psychological first aid. The intention was to add context to the special issue by providing a brief personal account of current clinical experience of dissociative responses to a trauma of which the readership would be very aware. Our correspondents were concerned that in providing this context the editorial did not comment on the broader situation in Gaza, which had become much worse by the time the paper copy of the journal was delivered.

We refute any suggestion that this was a deliberate and calculated decision to represent the discourse of the Israeli government or a Zionist perspective. However, we do recognise that the editorial made no mention of any context to the horrific attack of 7/10/23, such as the occupations of Gaza, the blockade or the illegal settler colonialism (Albanese, 2022). Nor was there any mention of the subsequent Palestinian deaths, which have now mounted to at least 34,622 and 77,867 injuries (OCHA, 2024). Kemp (2021, p. 37) argues that institutions such as this journal ‘have a responsibility to include in their reflections the constructive exercise of their public influence both in general terms and particularly in regard to the subjugation of the Palestinian people’. To this end, we would welcome the submission of articles that address these issues within an applied psychoanalytic framework.

We open this issue with an article entitled ‘Evaluating the 20-day Dynamic Interpersonal Therapy (DIT) training programme: a mixed methods investigation to identify characteristics associated with successful outcomes of DIT’. Jennifer Khalastchy, Deborah Abrahams, Rob Saunders and Peter Fonagy report on the new 20-day training format for DIT at the Anna Freud Centre in London based on the approved extended national curriculum for DIT training. This extended training is now being offered to counsellors and psychologists who do not meet the psychodynamic competencies required for the original five-day training. Trainees attend 20 face-to-face teaching days and receive close clinical supervision of three or four cases in order to acquire the required competencies to deliver DIT effectively. [[RunningHead]]

The Effectiveness of Psychodynamic Therapy in an NHS Psychotherapy Service: Outcomes for Service-Users with Complex Presentations

Rachel Hirschfeld , Scott Steen , E.L. Dunn , A. Hanif and L. Clarke

L. Clarke

Callum Lodge, 242 Lodge Road, Winson Green, Birmingham B18 5SJ, UK
Email: Rachel.hirschfeld@nhs.net

Hertfordshire, UK
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-6712-2761

Birmingham, UK

Coventry, UK

Edgbaston, Birmingham, UK

L. Clarke  

psychodynamic therapy, practice-based evidence, naturalistic study design, clinical practice, complex mental health problems

Complex and enduring mental health problems require greater treatment resources, usually in the form of multidisciplinary support, including providing psychological therapies. This paper reports on an NHS, tertiary-level specialist psychotherapy service offering Psychodynamic therapies with longer-term, exploratory transdiagnostic approaches to support complexity and sustained personality functioning. This paper adopts a naturalistic study design evaluating the effectiveness of Psychodynamic therapy using pre- and post-outcomes across a 10-year period. A total of n = 474 participants self-report pre- and post-outcome measures were used as the marker of effectiveness along with therapist assessments during intake and engagement. The findings showed that Psychodynamic therapy was effective in reducing psychological distress based on service-user self-report and therapist assessments. While intake scores varied by socio-demographic factors, the rate of change across most groups was similar. There were several limitations relating to data quality and completeness which reflect the naturalistic design. Despite the limits of a naturalistic design, this study provides evidence of support for the place of Psychodynamic therapies within NHS mental health care, catering to those with complex and enduring mental health problems.

[[RunningHead]]

Psychodynamic Therapists Treating Patients with Eating Disorders during COVID-19: Perceptions of the Therapeutic Relationship, Patient Experiences and Symptomatology, and Therapeutic Processes

Stephanie M. Morgan and Casey Firebaugh

Casey Firebaugh

Culver City, CA, USA
Email: Smorgan7@antioch.edu
http://orcid.org/0000-0003-3124-0835

Longmeadow, MA, USA
http://orcid.org/0000-0001-8015-0022

Casey Firebaugh  

COVID-19, eating disorders, psychodynamic, relational therapy, telehealth

Psychodynamic therapy is commonly used to treat patients with eating disorders. Patients with a diagnosed eating disorder have varying and complex treatment needs. Psychodynamic therapists transitioning their practices to telehealth during COVID-19 experienced the novelty of developing and sustaining a therapeutic connection with their patients without inhabiting a shared physical space. The aim of this qualitative phenomenological study was to gain insight into how psychodynamic therapists treating patients with eating disorders via telehealth during COVID-19 perceived their patients’ experiences and personally experienced the therapeutic relationship and therapeutic processes. Twelve respondents met inclusion criteria, consented to participate, provided demographic data, and participated in a 30-45 minute semi-structured interview. Results from the data analysis included four emergent themes: (1) depth of content; (2) manifestations of patient distress; (3) therapist-patient relationship; and (4) implementation of techniques. Participants spoke about the challenges and resilience experienced by themselves and their patients. They highlighted having to rapidly transition their therapy practice to serve patients with exacerbated symptoms. They shared the complexities of navigating a global pandemic and considered the delivery of relational and dynamic therapy via telehealth. Practical implications and considerations for future research are offered with an emphasis on the treatment of patients with eating disorders.

[[RunningHead]]

Talking to the Most Reasonable Voice: Building Rapport within a Crisis Service

Eveliina Ilola and Andrew John Howe

Andrew John Howe

London, UK
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-9512-1117

London, UK
Email: andrewjhowe@slam.nhs.uk
http://orcid.org/0000-0001-6688-6738

Andrew John Howe  

Carl Jung, Richard Lucas, psychosis, psychodynamic psychiatry, Home Treatment Team

This brief report concerns a patient interaction in a busy South London Home Treatment Team (HTT) service, where a psychodynamically informed approach was applied to a presentation of psychosis in a crisis setting. We discuss the case in light of the psychodynamic theories on psychosis of Carl Jung and Richard Lucas. Our patient, a young man, had been under the HTT for an extended period of time due to an episode of psychosis that was not resolving. After initially struggling to build rapport, we adapted Lucas’ and Jung’s models of interacting with acutely psychotic patients to re-establish psychic rapport, which led to our patient agreeing to take the medication that finally pulled him out of this long-standing episode. We believe this case highlights the power of psychodynamic thinking even in acute, short-term settings where extensive psychodynamic intervention may not be appropriate or feasible.

Poul Rohleder

Poul Rohleder

info@poulrohleder.com

Poul Rohleder  

This new book by Leezah Hertzmann and Juliet Newbigin is published as part of the Routledge Introductions to Contemporary Psychoanalysis book series. The books in this series are concise by design (word limit is fixed at 40,000 words) and offer a contemporary and accessible introduction to key topics, ideas and thinkers in psychoanalysis. Hertzmann, a couple and individual psychoanalytic psychotherapist, and Newbigin, a psychoanalytic psychotherapist, are editors of a previous volume, Sexuality and Gender Now: Moving Beyond Heteronormativity and are both previous recipients of the Bernard Rattigan Award for Psychoanalysis and Diversity, awarded by the British Psychoanalytic Council. They come, therefore, with a wealth of knowledge and experience, to bring us this book on psychoanalysis and homosexuality. Although a relatively short book, it manages to pack a lot in that is important, covering both historical ground, contemporary psychoanalytic thinking, and deep engagement with clinical material.

The book is structured into five chapters. The first three chapters provide an overview of the historical approaches to understanding homosexuality in psychoanalysis and how such approaches have shifted over time, from Freud’s somewhat open-minded understanding of homosexuality, to later theorising of homosexuality as pathological and perverse, to an eventual move to depathologising homosexuality and understanding it as part of normal sexual diversity. The last two chapters focus on contemporary ways of working clinically with gay, lesbian or bisexual patients.

The first chapter considers some of the historical, pre-Freud, conceptualisations of homosexuality and the work of some of the key sexologists working at the time that Freud began to develop his ideas. This is followed up in the second chapter, which describes the social and family context into which Freud was born, his upbringing and the social and professional context of his developing ideas. I confess that at first I was a little puzzled by how much space was taken up with outlining this historical context, given that the book is short. I felt myself a little impatient, wanting to get to contemporary psychoanalysis. However, what these two chapters do so clearly and importantly, is to show how the concept of ‘homosexuality’ and ‘the homosexual’ is inevitably shaped by biases related to prevailing social norms and values. Same-sex desire and same-sex sexual acts have always existed, but ‘homosexuality’ as conceptualised in the category of ‘the homosexual’ is relatively new (Fone, 2001). Misogyny in Psychoanalysis by Michaela Chamberlain, Phoenix Publishing House: London, UK 2022, 85 pp., £14.39 (pback), ISBN-13: 9781912691395

Annie Pesskin

Annie Pesskin

annie.pesskin@gmail.com

Annie Pesskin  

Since the Black Lives Matter movement, many psychoanalytic training committees have had to wrestle with how to de-colonise their curricula, as well as turn their listening ears to how doing a training feels different when you are B.A.M.E. On these issues the writings of Davids (2011), Dennis et al. (2020) have all been critical. Now, thanks to Michaela Chamberlain’s brave and pithy publication, Misogyny in Psychoanalysis, our field has another important avenue along which we can travel if we want to make psychoanalysis fit for purpose in its second century of life.

If the first century of psychoanalysis involved putting the unconscious firmly at the heart of human behaviour by using the powerful technique of free association to enliven and enrich our own and patients’ lives, perhaps our second century will see us working towards making psychoanalysis - in theory, practice and training - a welcoming place for people of all classes, ethnicities and genders.

Chamberlain’s book is small but mighty, in that into her five short chapters she packs such a fascinating and diverse range of subjects, all of which circle around the key question, ‘How does misogyny hide in plain sight in psychoanalysis?’ Her journey into this fraught domain began over a decade ago when she was working as an honorary psychotherapist for a London NHS Trust. Every month, her patient went psychotic for just a few days when she ovulated. Chamberlain went to her supervisor asking for papers relating to menstruation expecting her helpful supervisor to be her usual fount of knowledge. But instead there was a long pause, followed by, ‘You write such a paper and I will help you edit it’.

Shocked by the evident gap in interest in the female experience of menstruation, Chamberlain then stumbled on area after area where misogyny appeared rampant, yet seemed to hide in plain sight. She wondered why it was that psychoanalysts such as Karen Horney, then later on Juliet Mitchell and Jessica Benjamin among them, who took issue with Freudian theories of female sexual development as stamped with loss and suffused by lack, were dismissed as ‘feminist’ authors so that their contributions ended up relegated to a ‘niche’ area which left the psychoanalytic canon untouched. She noticed how the word ‘feminine’ was used as a synonym for submission, how no male theorist had taken up the mantle of exploring Freud’s ‘dark continent’ since his day, and how the fundamental experiences of pregnancy, birth, menopause and menstruation were almost always overlooked in the literature (a notable exception being Joan Raphael Leff’s excellent 2014 contribution). [[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Paul Cundy

Paul Cundy

London, UK
paulcundy@protonmail.com

Paul Cundy  

We begin this issue with an article by Sarah Ramsey, titled ‘When Florence met Freud: interaction and intersection between psychoanalysis and nursing’. The paper begins with an overview of literature from the last century which has applied psychoanalytic theory to various aspects of the nursing profession. The literature has used this psychoanalytic lens to examine a broad range of subjects such as the motivation to enter the career, stress and burnout, the nurse-patient relationship, role and task, transference, research, Lacan, the impact of societal change on the profession as well as health service processes and systems. Ramsey provides an overview of each of these areas, focusing on general hospital nursing in the UK’s National Health Service, but including the work of nurses in psychiatric and international settings where relevant. She offers a critical perspective on the application of psychoanalytic theory ‘outside the clinic’. The limited engagement between psychoanalysis and general nursing is considered from both perspectives. The author highlights the potential for an enriching dialogue between the two disciplines, as well as exploring potential barriers.

The second paper, ‘Auditory hallucinations in non-psychotic disorders - an analytical psychological perspective’ is by Andrew Howe, Arsime Demjaha and Kevin Lu. The authors make use of Jung’s Complex Theory to explore the experience of hallucinations in people with diagnoses of Schizophrenia or Borderline Personality Disorder. They draw on the concept of ego strength as a mediator of Complexes. Using a Neuropsychological model of the ego they point to differences in neuroactivity which may lead to hallucinations being experienced either externally or internally. They argue that this would support Jung’s introversion/extraversion - psychosis/hysteria hypothesis. They conclude that this Jungian formulation of hallucinations is highly relevant to contemporary healthcare as it offers an explanatory model of the manifestation of hallucinations in multiple conditions, not just psychosis.

[[RunningHead]]

Auditory Hallucinations in Non-Psychotic Disorders - An Analytical Psychological Perspective

Andrew John Howe , Arsime Demjaha and Kevin Lu

Kevin Lu

London, UK
Email: andrewjhowe@doctors.net.uk
http://orcid.org/0000-0001-6688-6738

Colchester, UK

http://orcid.org/0000-0002-1870-6410

London, UK
http://orcid.org/0000-0001-5512-9635

Kevin Lu  

Jung, hallucinations, psychosis, schizophrenia, personality disorder

Although hallucinations are a feature of psychosis, they can present in non-psychotic disorders and may occur in non-pathological states. Jung argues that unconscious complexes underpin hallucinations and further observes that some of the symptoms of ‘hysteric’ patients - including hallucinations - were also common amongst patients with schizophrenia. However, the outward presentation of symptoms was markedly different for each patient group. Jung mobilises his complex theory to explain this difference. We argue that Jung’s understanding of hallucinations applies to contemporary healthcare; it frames how hallucinations may manifest in multiple conditions, not just psychosis. This brief report discusses Jung’s theories and their continued veracity in contemporary contexts.

[[RunningHead]]

Mentalization-Based Supervision in Environmental Therapy: A Guide to Mentalizing Chaos in Residential Care and Inpatient Treatment

Marie Bloch

Marie Bloch

Aalborg, Denmark
Email: psykologmariebloch@gmail.com
http://orcid.org/0009-0008-3859-2255

Marie Bloch  

mentalization, environmental therapy, MBT, supervision, inpatient treatment

This paper is the first to present a model for mentalization-based supervision of professionals working with environmental therapy. Mentalization-based supervision should be based on a mentalizing stance and theory aligned with the current mentalizing perspective, that is, the theory of attachment, mentalization and should aim at establishing ‘we-mode’ and epistemic trust. This paper offers a practical guide, models, and templates that are easy to apply and beneficial for the supervisor to underpin the mentalizing stance and focus on mentalizing processes during complex supervision sessions with groups of supervisees. The approach and method of supervision are demonstrated through a clinical case, and the supervision process is illustrated via templates to aid the reader who is a supervisor for groups in this field. The article is based on current theory and research on mentalization and on the clinical practice of supervision in psychiatric inpatient treatment and residential care.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reading Lacan Without Giving Up: Some Organizing Principles to Help Orient Psychotherapists to the Lacanian Tradition of Psychoanalysis

John Garrett Tanner

John Garrett Tanner

Berkeley, CA, USA
http://orcid.org/0000-0002-5105-8099
4515 Menchaca Road, Suite 204, Austin, TX 78745, USA
Email: jgtannerphd@gmail.com

John Garrett Tanner  

Lacan, clinic, knowledge, love, frustration

This article is a primer to reading the primary texts of Jacques Lacan. I argue that there is an experiential dimension to the act of reading Lacanian texts that approximates the transmission of psychoanalysis in a clinical setting. A primary insistence is that Lacanian psychoanalysis has a fundamentally different relationship to knowledge than other therapeutic and theoretical traditions, and that therefore any emphasis on understanding is incongruous with the larger aims of the project. I discuss Lacanian conceptions of frustration and love in order to explore these ideas, before turning to a specific moment in the Lacanian corpus. I conclude by exploring implications for work in the public sector.

Rael Meyerowitz

Rael Meyerowitz

rael.meyerowitz@gmail.com

Rael Meyerowitz  

It is a privilege to have been asked to review this momentous publication, something that the psychoanalytic world has been anticipating for a long time now - far too long for the expression ‘with bated breath’ to have any realistic application here: no one could hold their breath for that long!

For more than one reason, it is a particular pleasure to review this updated version of the Standard Edition. When I trained at the BPAS, Mark Solms was one of my Freud teachers - by some distance the best of them. I have since gone on to be a frequent Freud teacher myself, in various psychoanalytic contexts, and I have always kept the folder containing my notes from his seminars close at hand. Even before becoming an analyst, my educational and academic path through literature and philosophy provided access to many impressively erudite versions of Freud: those of Lionel Trilling, Phillip Reiff, Harold Bloom, Jacques Lacan, Paul Ricoeur, Joseph Sandler, Richard Wollheim, Jonathan Lear, to name just some. Solms’ Freud is as expansive and intellectually stimulating as these, but with the additional benefit of how seriously he takes Freud’s claim to being a scientist of the mind. Solms has now, of course, become a world-renowned neuroscientist in his own right, a development that might well have carried him far afield, well away from anything to do with psychoanalysis. This long-awaited publication of the RSE (there is also a companion publication of Freud’s neuroscientific writings in the works) is as much evidence as could be required that he is still flying the psychoanalytic flag; it remains his mission not to break, but to consolidate and further forge, the intimate connections between the Freudian mind and the scientific brain.

A perhaps more personal reason has to do with the fact that Solms and I are fellow South Africans and I am frankly proud that someone from our background can now be credited with producing this new version of Freud’s writings. Moreover, during approximately the same period, Solms worked hard to consolidate and integrate the somewhat scattered and fragmented psychodynamic scene in South Africa, helping to turn it into the thriving psychoanalytic community that now exists there. He and I grew up in a country in which a heinously racist political regime regarded the teachings of Freud and his followers with almost as much suspicion and disdain as those of Marx. Ann Hughes

Ann Hughes

ms.annhughes@gmail.com

Ann Hughes  

This book is a collection of papers initially offering a thorough and often painful and troubling exploration of therapists’ experiences of Complaints Procedures. As the editors, Dr Adah Sachs and Dr Valerie Sinason, state in the Introduction ‘This book was born out of complaints both editors went through (and were acquitted of)’. Later chapters focus on the historical perspective, whilst the penultimate chapter offers a harsh critique of current complaint procedures maintaining they ‘are often anti-therapeutic and unethical in their treatment of therapists’. This latter stance is firmly echoed in the final chapter which concludes ‘The current system of investigation over-promises and under-delivers’. Overall, this book is comprised of powerful and wounding clinical papers, contextual history and strident and well researched informed analysis of the failings in the Complaints Procedures operated by voluntary and statutory regulators all under the wing of the Professional Standards Authority (PSA).

There is acknowledgement that some complaints raised do prove that the patient has suffered or been wronged. The emphasis, however, is more on the wronged therapist. This is unlike the recent article in Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy ‘The experience of making a complaint in a case of professional misconduct: towards a safer profession’ by John Hook and Intra Fines (2023), which gives greater focus to the patient raising the complaint. The same powerful feelings and distress, however, caused throughout the processes are echoed; anxiety, shame and humiliation dominating respective participants. Visceral feelings and suffering are the result of complaint processes whichever side one is occupying.

The Hook and Fines article outlines that the pain of the complainant is compounded by the experience of not being fully validated; that they had to prove they had been harmed. Similarly in this book constant references were made by therapists feeling they were treated as ‘guilty until proven innocent’ in contrast to Article 6 Human Rights Act ‘Everyone charged with a criminal offence shall be presumed innocent until proven guilty’. Unfortunately, the therapists’ position, once accused, would be very much like any public figure who is ‘named and shamed’ over a range of accusations from bullying to sexual harassment to financial impropriety. The reverberations for the shocked therapists would of course be extremely concerning and weighty as they all state. 261

Jade Wright  

I was drawn to this book as the topic of how trauma travels through time interests me. As a CT3 in psychiatry currently working in a psychotherapy department, I have seen in my own work the way that relational patterns are replicated generation after generation. The book struck me as accessible due the structure of relating theories to real life occurrences, using many widely known examples.

Fromm draws upon many of the atrocities of our recent history and looks at how the trauma of the events lives on, in both society and the individual. Not only does this approach make the psychoanalytic concepts accessible, it also gives the reader a deeper understanding of history and how it may affect the reader and the people we interact with.

The book is broken into three parts: trauma in families, organizations and society. Various important and unsettling events of history are addressed: the rise of Jihad, 9/11, clusters of college student suicides, and PTSD affecting ex-military persons. Attention is given to both the historical trauma that forms part of the context for these events, alongside the ongoing lasting effect of the events.

Perhaps the most gripping chapter for me takes the story of Christopher Johnson McCandless, a 24 year old man who ventures into the wild and ultimately dies. Having watched the cinematic portrayal of this story in the film ‘Into the Wild’, I was really able to connect with this chapter. Fromm’s analysis of the conscious and unconscious beliefs that led Christopher to depart upon his tragic journey transformed my perception from an adventure gone wrong to, as Fromm describes it, ‘an identity crisis ultimately becoming an identity end’.

Another chapter that stood out for me was ‘Erik Erikson and the “cogwheeling” of life cycles’. Having studied Erikson’s eight stages of psychosocial development, this chapter gave an insight into the life and experiences that influenced Erikson’s theories. Fromm, who directed the Erikson Institute at the Austen Riggs Centre in Chicago for many years, describes Erikson’s own scattered identity, arguing the importance of Erikson’s concept of identity crisis: ‘for him it was personal’. Fromm argues that Erikson’s own ‘life story of healing’ was influenced by wishing to adapt to his mother’s needs of being healed, ‘intertwined with … transmission of [her] own traumatic experiences’. Annie Pesskin  

But why it is that this detachment of libido from its objects should be such a painful process is a mystery to us and we have not hitherto been able to frame any hypothesis to account for it. (Freud, 1917e).

I have a hunch that the author of this wonderful book, the London-based literature Professor-turned-Psychoanalyst, Rael Meyerowitz, might not argue with me if I asserted that the quotation about mourning from Freud (above) was the diving board he used to launch himself into the pool of psychoanalytic writing which concerns itself with how we manage loss, or more pertinently, for those of us who spend our days listening to unhappy people, how some spectacularly fail to manage it.

Another way of putting his theme is this: by what metapsychological means do we mourn and work through our losses, and, indeed, how do we glean or gain something internally valuable from that assimilatory process? And when this goes awry, what forms of psychic indigestion do we commonly find amongst our suffering patients in the consulting room?

Meyerowitz describes this book as a ‘textual tussle’ with the psychoanalytic thinkers who have offered us the best opportunities, in his opinion, to metabolise mourning. Their works are ‘tussled’ with, in the sense of being poked and prodded, described and disordered, in seven fascinating chapters, featuring in order of appearance: Freud, Ferenczi, Abraham, Klein, Loewald, Abraham and Torok and André Green. Hans Loewald is the only American influence, while some helpful French, Lacan-influenced ideas are brought to the party by André Green and his influencers, Nicolas Abraham and Maria Torok.

In addition to psychoanalysts, there are some other important people invited to this feast. They are Meyerowitz’s chosen writers, William Wordsworth, Sylvia Plath, Wallace Stevens and most importantly, William Faulkner. It is for this reason that before you settle down to read his book, you must first whet your appetite with Faulkner’s hors d’oeuvre, A Rose for Emily (Faulkner, 1930), which is a macabre and unsettling tale of pathological mourning told in a pithy 3,700 words (available for free online). Therapists sitting with their patients are much like the townspeople walking past Miss Emily’s house - we are so curious but we cannot know what goes on inside except at particularly revealing moments. Like the inhabitants of this Mississippi town, we must watch and wait - and hope the patient will change. The process is really so opaque!

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Intractable International Conflicts: Psychodynamic Understanding and hope for Resolution

Allan Abbass , Yuval Alon , Johannes Ermagan , Jack Tawil and Reza Zarabi

Reza Zarabi

Halifax, Canada
Email: Allan.Abbass@Dal.Ca

Tel Aviv, Israel

Sweden

Knoxville, MD, USA

Drammen, Norway

Reza Zarabi  

psychodynamic, short-term, military conflict, peace, community building

With a backdrop of chronic military conflict in the Middle East, a group of practitioners and trainers in Intensive Short-term Dynamic Psychotherapy (ISTDP) with roots in Israel, Lebanon, Palestine, Iran and Assyria (Turkey) provide commentary using this model to understand some factors in the precipitation, perpetuation and possible resolution of intractable conflict. First, we review the metapsychology and treatment methods of ISTDP. We explore the ISTDP understanding of attachment trauma as a driver of self-destructive behavior and interactional patterns that leave populations vulnerable to chronic conflict. We review external factors which impinge upon healthy attachments and family and system wellbeing. We review a way of understanding how attachment trauma at one level of the system may influence other levels of the system and vice versa. Finally, we attempt to consolidate this by considering how intrapsychic processes may influence and inform intrafamilial, intracommunity, intranational and international healing between conflicted parties.
